data.title: Nucor Earnings: Investing In Quality Makes A Difference
data.description:
Investing in high-quality businesses makes a big difference in both good and bad times for equities. The past 12-month period was yet another exceptional year for equities, with the S&P 500 increasing by almost a third.

The past year was also a very good one for investing in the U.S. steel sector, as tariffs were introduced on raw steel, aluminum, and copper, as well as some derivative products. This, however, did not mean that all U.S. steel producers would deliver similar returns, with Nucor (NUE) leading the pack with an 86% price increase and Cleveland-Cliffs (CLF) coming in last with a 34% increase.

The difference in the market's reaction between Nucor's Q1 2026 results and those of its major peer, Cleveland-Cliffs, is also an indication of what investors perceive as quality in the sector. Even though quarterly noise is definitely at play, NUE is up by around 5% following its recent release, while CLF went in the other direction following the release of its Q1 results.

But before we go into further detail on Nucor's Q1 figures, we should also mention that the stock offers significantly lower risk when compared to its U.S. peers.

NUE's standard deviation of daily returns, for example, is more than twice as low as that of CLF over the past year. The metric is often used as a measure for total risk, and in that regard, NUE is much more likely to preserve shareholder returns.

Nucor's beta is also much lower than that of CLF and is the lowest within the peer group. Beta is used as a measure of market risk, and once again it seems that NUE is in a much better position to shelter shareholder returns, if market-wide forces take a turn for the worse.

Key Takeaways From Q1 Earnings

Nucor reported results that beat analysts' expectations both on the top and bottom lines, but that wasn't what prompted the market to react positively following the release.

One thing that immediately stands out when comparing NUE's earnings call to the one of CLF is that there's significantly less emphasis being put on outcomes of political decisions and tariffs. Fundamentals improved across the board with earnings noting a large increase and free cash flow benefiting both from higher margins and a gradual reduction of the company's record-high capital expenditures.

Average selling prices fell slightly sequentially, but the year-on-year increase is still significant, and gross margins continued to increase. As a result, it now appears highly likely that FY 2024-25 marked a bottom in gross margins, and Nucor is on track to return to its prior levels of profitability.

With average selling prices bound to continue improving in 2026, provided that the economy avoids a major slowdown, Nucor's margins would also continue to benefit from higher shipments. The Q1 report provided strong evidence of demand accelerating as steel mills backlogs grew by 20% and steel products increased by 9%.

For comparison, quarter-on-quarter growth in mill backlog was 10% in the prior quarter (Q4 of 2025).

Management also anticipates higher input cost headwinds to ease as the year progresses and higher average selling prices offset the cost pressures.

We did see some margin compression due to higher steel input costs flowing through, but we expect this to ease as the year progresses and realized pricing catches up.

Source: Nucor Q1 2026 Earnings Transcript

Moreover, market participants were also pleasantly surprised by Nucor's management optimism that the rest of 2026 is likely to play-out better than initially expected. So far, management anticipates more than 5% growth in shipments, which at first glance is lower than the prior period.

After achieving approximately 6% growth of shipments in 2025, we expect shipments to grow by more than 5% in 2026.

Source: Nucor Q1 2026 Earnings Transcript

However, the commentary extract we see below has confirmed that NUE's management is likely being very conservative in their estimates, and most likely we will see an increase in shipment growth over the one reported in 2025.

To your point on the 5%, yes, I think you're right. I think it's much more likely that it pushes closer to double digits. Again, not ready to say it's going to be at or above 10%, but we -- I think it will strongly be above that 5% mark.

Source: Nucor Q1 2026 Earnings Transcript

As a result of all that, Nucor's earnings per share increased significantly during the quarter, and this trend would continue.

Therefore, provided that the steel market does not experience an adverse idiosyncratic event in 2025, NUE appears to be very well-positioned to deliver earnings above current expectations.

Thus the current price-to-earnings multiple of around 16 on a forward basis could prove to be conservative over the coming months.

Conclusion

Nucor's Q1 2026 earnings report did not disappoint, and once again we see evidence of why it is not worth chasing higher returns by investing in lower-quality businesses and taking additional risk. While there are certainly some risks related to the U.S. economy in 2026, NUE is now in a very good spot to increase margins and realize higher-than-expected shipments. The stellar share price performance from the past year will likely moderate, but overall NUE is still positioned to outperform its peers and the equity market as a whole. I rate shares a "Buy."

data.title: NIE: Well Supported Dividend Yield At An Attractive Valuation
data.description:
Overview

When I previously covered the Virtus Equity & Convertible Income Fund (NIE), I issued a buy rating due to the attractive valuation and return potential at the time. Since then, the share price has experienced some volatility but the total return remains positive. The fund has released an updated annual report for its 2026 period, so I wanted to revisit the fund's earnings performance, dividend stability, and outlook for the remainder of the year. I believe that NIE can deliver attractive returns going forward if the market momentum remains positive.

At the time of my last coverage, NIE traded at a discount to NAV of 10.07%. Following the slight pullback in the market through the first quarter of the year, NIE now trades at a slightly larger discount to NAV of 10.64%. Referring to the red line on the graph below, NIE now trades at a higher-than-average price to NAV level. For reference, NIE has actually traded at an average discount to NAV of 10.07% over the last five years. While this can be a solid time to accumulate, there are still some downside risks to consider.

The fund is very reliant on technology stocks and a downturn in the sector can be disastrous for NIE. This can put NIE in a position where it is paying out more than it earns, which can hurt the growth of the underlying NAV. NIE currently offers a starting dividend yield of about 7.8%, while issuing its payouts on a quarterly basis. The latest annual report confirms that NIE can support the distributions with ease, but the portfolio is very reliant on net realized gains.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, NIE has total managed assets of $795.6M that are spread across a blended portfolio of assets. The fund's primary goal is to generate attractive total returns through a combination of capital appreciation and income generation. To achieve this goal, the fund combines common equities and convertible securities. Convertible securities as a sort of hybrid instrument that operates like a bond, but management can convert those positions to common shares if they believe the position has attractive upside potential.

  • Common Equities: 54.98%
  • Convertible Securities: 39.28%
  • Cash and Equivalents: 5.76%

As you may already know, there aren't many equities that can support NIE's high dividend yield. Therefore, the fund implements an option writing strategy against a portion of its assets to help amplify the income level that is generated within its holdings. This allows NIE to collect option premiums, which are then used to support the distributions paid. The fund limits the option writing to 70% of the value of each stock position, but this can shift based on the market environment.

NIE also provides instant diversification across different industries, however, there may be some vulnerability related to its concentration to technology companies. The fund is most heavily allocated to information technology companies at 34.51% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to health care and financials, which accounts for 10.7% and 10.1% of assets respectively.

When assessing the top ten positions, we can see that NIE has exposure to many high quality businesses. However, the focus on technology also makes sense because it allows the option writing strategy to generate higher premiums from the sector's volatility. Nvidia (NVDA) is the largest holding at 5.37% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to other notable companies, including Apple (AAPL), Alphabet (GOOG), Microsoft (MSFT), and Amazon (AMZN), just to name a few. So the fund does hold positions that do not pay a dividend, which is aligned for the goal of capturing price appreciation.

Performance Expectations

Since my last coverage, NIE released an updated annual report for its 2026 period. The fund's timing around its fiscal year is a bit weird, so the year ends in January. Net investment income amounted to $0.12 per share, while net realized $4.01 per share. Looking back over the last few years, we can see that net investment income fell within the bottom end of its historical range. Net investment income consists of the dividends and interest received from its positions, so I don't expect this to be high. However, the poor net investment income levels means that the fund is reliant on net realized gains to support most of its earnings.

The reliance on net realized gains are common for equity focused closed-end funds, but I would like to see net investment income levels increase in the future. This will increase the underlying NAV stability and provide investors with healthier dividend support during periods of market declines. The current dynamic means that the fund needs positive market momentum so that it can sell securities at a higher valuation than they were purchased for.

This can be an issue during periods of declines because NIE will likely fail to produce net realized gains that outpace distributions. This was the case during 2022's market decline, which negatively impacted the NAV. For instance, NIE reported net realized losses of $4.02 per share for its 2023 period and the fund still hasn't been able to get back to its prior NAV level several years later. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts over the last few years:

  • 2022: $30.32 per share
  • 2023: $22.43 per share
  • 2024: $23.82 per share
  • 2025: $26.52 per share
  • 2026: $28.12 per share

A reliance on net realized gains also means that the fund has to actively sell off the upside growth of its positions to prioritize distributions. Therefore, its positions cannot simply grow over time without interruption. When you combine this with the included option writing strategy, investors should limit their expectations for NIE to provide price appreciation. For instance, the fund's share price has increased by slightly more than 41.1%, despite market indices racing to all-time highs over the last decade. When including all distributions paid out to investors, the total return jumps up to 270.9% over the same time frame.

Looking forward, I believe that NIE will continue to provide attractive returns, especially as the technology market continues to expand. However, it is important to understand the exact use case for NIE. The fund isn't structurally built to provide the maximum level of total returns. Instead, the fund is optimized for income generation and the included option writing writing means that NIE is best used as an accompanying position.

For instance, you may already hold a portfolio of growth-focused equities or ETFs. NIE would pair well because you are still able to partially participate in upside movements, but the fund will essentially extract a layer of income from positions you may already hold. While traditional investors are stuck waiting for market recoveries, NIE can print cash flow even when markets are trading sideways.

This was the case over the last six months, while markets trended sideways. The S&P 500 ETF (SPY) traded sideways for the last six months because of the selloff in software companies and rising geopolitical uncertainty. Over the last six months, SPY's total return sits around 4.2%. Meanwhile, NIE's total return was a bit higher at 5.7% over the same time frame, likely attributed to the income generation that serves as a buffer against sideways markets.

Risk

Since the fund is most heavily allocated to technology stocks, NIE is going to demonstrate some sensitivity to the issues within the sector. For instance, the market continues to punish software companies due to the rising threat of AI. The capabilities and cost-efficiency of AI tools have become so impressive that investors are now concerned that the moat many software companies had are now eliminated. Furthermore, the market is skeptical of the large capex spending levels of the hyperscalers.

While I am optimistic about the outlook of the technology sector, these short-term challenges can quickly shift momentum. If the market is unpleased with the rate of earnings growth, this can spark a deeper selloff across the indices, which would also negatively impact NIE's performance. In this scenario, the fund will be unable to capture net realized gains and the underlying NAV may erode.

Furthermore, the fund's high dividend yield doesn't automatically mean that NIE can act as a hedge against declines. In fact, the scenario of paying out more than it earns is likely to exacerbate the downside risks. This vulnerability was demonstrated through 2022's market decline. SPY saw a total loss of 18.17% during the year, but NIE suffered from a much larger total loss of 26.7% over the same time frame, despite offering a much higher dividend yield.

Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared quarterly distribution of $0.50 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 7.7% Assuming NIE keeps distributions unchanged for the remainder of the year, we can estimate an annual payout amount of $2.00 per share. Referring back to the same annual report, it is clear that NIE is capable of generating earnings that outpace the distributions that are being paid. For instance, NIE reported total earnings of $4.13 per share for the year, which was large enough to support two years worth of payouts. Therefore, I am not necessarily worried about the overall sustainability of distributions.

Over the last few years, management has chosen to distribute those excess earnings as a supplemental distribution. This can be great for income investors but it then leaves the fund's NAV vulnerable if the earnings are weak in the following year. I think supplementals can be a great bonus over time, but I think it comes at the cost of the NAV growth over time. I wouldn't be opposed to management reserving most of its excess earnings to help boost the NAV growth over time.

Since the fund is reliant on net realized gains, NIE is actively selling off the growth of its strongest positions. I believe that the latest available section19(a) notice confirms this. For instance, 96.9% of the distributions paid on a YTD basis were classified as net realized short-term capital gains. This classified of distribution has the highest tax burden for investors, so the fund may not offer any tax-efficiency. For this reason, a position in NIE would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors that want to minimize their overall tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on NIE at this time. The fund trades at a reasonable price to NAV valuation and I believe the portfolio strategy can continue to participate in some upside growth of the market, while also generating income from its option writing strategy. The fund provides instant diversification across the highest quality companies in the world, while offering dividend income stability for investors. However, investors should be aware that the distributions do not offer tax-efficiency and the NAV growth may be unsteady during market declines.

data.title: Kiniksa: Increased Outlook For Arcalyst, Augmented By Pipeline Maturation
data.description:
An update on Kiniksa Pharmaceuticals (KNSA) is long overdue. Since my last article in June 2025, the company made strong commercial progress with Arcalyst exceeding expectations, again, while the pipeline was in clinical trial execution phase.

The commercial execution was so good that even my increased valuation range of $43-$45 turned out quite conservative as it was based on Arcalyst net sales of $900-$950 million in 2027, a range that will likely be exceeded this year.

Kiniksa is up 91% since my previous update, and it is up 441% since I added it to Growth Stock Forum’s Coverage Universe in March 2022.

I have largely stopped using valuation as an anchor of my investment thesis as I found that I tend to leave money on the table by adhering to what nearly always turns out to be a conservative model. At least for high quality growth names, and those are the ones that matter the most.

For the purposes of continuity here, the new valuation range on Kiniksa would be $57-$63 per share, up from $43-$45 per share, based on next year’s net sales of Arcalyst of $1.2-$1.3 billion.

The range does not give credit to the emerging next-generation pipeline candidates KPL-387 and KPL-1161 that have significantly better economics for Kiniksa and they could add considerably to the net present value. For these reasons, I will remain bullish on Kiniksa, above and beyond that valuation range on Arcalyst.

Arcalyst’s outlook improves yet again, and the story is still early

Arcalyst has been making steady progress since launch and has captured 18% of the recurrent pericarditis market at the end of 2025, and Kiniksa only counts multiple recurrences here, leaving the larger first recurrence market largely untapped.

As I covered before, the major growth driver for the drug has been the duration of therapy. At launch, I had less than a year of duration of therapy as the basis for sales calculations and modeling, and the average duration has steadily increased to reach approximately three years as physicians have adopted the theory that this is a multiyear disease with approximately one third of patients suffering from recurrences at year 5 of their disease.

In my June 2025 article, I argued that the beat-and-raise will continue throughout the year and that Arcalyst net sales will likely exceed $620 million versus the company’s $590-$605 million guidance range at the time. And beat and raise it did as Arcalyst’s net sales reached $677.6 million.

I expect more of the same this year and my base case is net sales north of $970 million, and a bull case of $1 billion or somewhat more. Below are the updated charts with sequential net sales changes since launch and estimates for the rest of 2026 based on the mid-point of the company’s increased guidance range of $937.5 million and my bull case of $1 billion (my estimates for quarterly sales in both cases). To get to $1 billion in net sales this year, Arcalyst’s absolute sequential net sales changes need to be similar to what they were at the end of 2025 and the start of 2026 on a four-quarter trailing basis, and I believe this is achievable.

The additional growth driver this year should be the recently launched consumer awareness campaign.

KPL-387 to deliver proof-of-concept data in the second half of 2026

The extremely dry period for Kiniksa’s clinical pipeline is reaching its end as the company expects to report the topline results from the phase 2 part of the phase 2/3 trial of KPL-387 in recurrent pericarditis patients in the second half of the year.

As a reminder, this is the next-generation IL-1 antibody candidate (Arcalyst’s successor) designed for monthly administration through a convenient subcutaneous injection versus weekly administration of Arcalyst.

Kiniksa hedged its bets in the phase 2 trial by testing four different doses of KPL-387, including dosing every two weeks along with monthly administration, but pharmacokinetic data in healthy volunteers suggests monthly coverage can be achieved with a single 300mg dose, and potentially even with the 100mg dose.

Arcalyst has set a high bar for efficacy with a 96% reduction in recurrence, and as such, I only expect KPL-387 to be in line on efficacy when compared across trials with at least two of the four dose groups that are being tested – 300mg monthly and 300mg bi-weekly and potentially 100mg bi-weekly. The coverage of the 100mg monthly dose is probably insufficient to achieve high efficacy, and it is probably there to satisfy the dose-ranging requirements.

The development program of KPL-387 is designed for a seamless transition from phase 2 to phase 3, and the phase 3 part is expected to start before the end of 2026.

The implications of improved convenience for patients are significant, and so are the economics for Kiniksa. Instead of a 50:50 profit split on Arcalyst with Regeneron (REGN), Kiniksa gets to keep all the profits on KPL-387 to itself. And that would be significant since Regeneron's part of the collaboration profit was $229 million in 2025 and $75.6 million in Q1 2026.

Based on the Arcalyst economics, I expect that $1 billion in net sales of KPL-387 would increase Kiniksa's operating profit by close to $350 million.

Conclusion

Kiniksa is a clean Arcalyst execution story with the upside from next-generation pipeline candidates with greatly improved economics for the company and potentially improved convenience for recurrent pericarditis patients.

The main risks in the near-term are Arcalyst underperforming expectations (this risk seems very low) and KPL-387 generating disappointing phase 2 results later this year.

Longer-term, Kiniksa could see increased competition in the recurrent pericarditis market, mainly from Eli Lilly (LLY) with VTX2735 it gained through the acquisition of Ventyx last year and which is in phase 2 development for recurrent pericarditis. There is also Monte Rosa Therapeutics (GLUE) with its NEK7 molecular glue degrader MRT-8102, which has similar activity to IL-1 antibodies in preclinical models, and which is orally available. However, Monte Rosa is significantly behind (even behind Kiniksa’s KPL-387) and is yet to start developing MRT-8102 for the treatment of pericarditis.

Disclaimer: This article reflects the author's opinion and should not be regarded as a buy or sell recommendation or investment advice in any way.

data.title: Digital Realty Preferreds: Strong Demand Prospects And Discount To Par
data.description:
The preferred stocks of Digital Realty Trust (DLR) is the recent addition I found worth discussing about. While the company itself, being involved in data center operations is at an interesting phase, with favorable macro backdrop, which directly benefits common stocks units, however the reason to focus in preferred shares at present is largely to do with the differentiation in current dividend distributions yields accompanied by a slightly lesser risk profile

The company has three issues listed on the exchange- Series J (DLR.PR.J) Series K (DLR.PR.K) and Series L (DLR.PR.L). I will however focus primarily with respect two Series J and Series L because of their wider discount from par value of $25. The Series J is trading at ~$20.3, with Series K at ~$22.8 and Series L at ~$20.06, similar to Series J. Therefore, the gap between par value of $25 stands meaningfully reduced for Series K as compared to other two series of preferred shares.

The common stock unit of DLR presently offers a dividend yield of ~2.5% on a trailing basis on market price. The said yield is reduced somewhat due to the recent price appreciation on the commons. The common stock for that matter is up by ~25% for this calendar year so far, while the preferreds are trading at slightly below their beginning of the year values.

The preferred shares, therefore for a relatively low risk seeking, income focused investors appear to be comparatively attractive. That does not disregard the case for commons altogether, but a far greater emphasis on qualitative developments and business prospects shall be placed upon when allocating to commons. An income focused investor in addition might want to go with a distribution yield which is more than twice of that of a common, while still having some optionality of upside intact in case of interest rate declines. I recently covered the preferred shares of UMH properties (UMH), a REIT operating in manufactured housing and rated those as a buy. The preferred shares of DLR are yet another complimentary addition which an investor could look into, considering taking into account their overall asset allocations.

Company Overview

DLR primarily operates as a REIT focusing on operating data center platforms. This is how the company summarizes its business.

“We provide a global data center platform that supports our customers’ digital infrastructure and enables our customers to interconnect with their customers and partners. We solve global coverage, capacity and connectivity needs for companies of all sizes, including the world’s leading enterprises and services providers, through PlatformDIGITAL®, a global data center platform for scaling digital business which enables customers to deploy their critical infrastructure with a global data center provider”. The company is a provider of data center, colocation and interconnection solutions for customers across a variety of industries.

The company is well capitalized with a common equity capital of $22.6 billion, sitting on an asset base of Around ~$49 billion. In addition, the preferred base is relatively fairly small standing at a ~$755 million. Unlike some companies who keep a large stack of preferred stocks on their equity capital, and gets limited in its flexibility of distributions, in current case, the proportion of preference shares in capital structure stands fairly small. The debt obligations stand around ~$17.5-$18 billions.

The magnitude of total assets by itself suggests the scope of the operations of the company. This is not a regional player, but consists of operations in multiple jurisdictions. While the company had around ~$17.5 billion in debt obligations at the end of FY25, increased from approximately ~$15.5 billion a year prior, however the maturity profile of the debt is stable with no near- term stress visible for the present times. The weighted average coupon sits remarkably low, however, post refinancing that would may possibly tend to drift upwards.

With reference to qualitative aspects, the prospects are fairly constructive at present. I keep highlighting the current trajectory of infrastructure ancillary/ AI enablers, which stand to benefit far more than the ones spending enormous amount of capital in form of growth capital expenditures. The underlying physical assets such as data centers, power units etc. usually possess a relatively far stable source of revenue stream than the big tech companies that are presently operating in an intense competitive market, with backing of largely intangible assets. This physical infrastructure in contrast to intangible technology, cannot be developed overnight, also given the underlying nature of those assets and constraints such as land, power, permit, regulatory clearance etc just to name a few. The demand outlook for the data center and digital enabling assets remain fairly strong at present. The below graph suggests the spending of just top few large technology companies’ capital expenditures to exceed $600 billion in a few years.

The acceleration of backlog for DLR further reflects on this aspect. The ~$1.8 billion of backlog suggested by the company highlights the value of the assets they possess, along with the visibility of the stream of rents and FFO which enables the company to fund distributions to its capital providers. On an overall basis, considering the strong demand tailwinds, especially in digital AI enabling infrastructure, where the demand happens to be most and the very sector which the company caters to, tends to enhance the business prospects of the company.

Company Preference Shares

With respect to Preferred shares, naturally any strong macro backdrop tends to support these securities as well, by increasing the cushion and stability. However, the important aspect is that eventually the preferred holder would be receiving the contracted payouts, the benefit of any business prospects primarily and largely would be attributable to common equity holders. Therefore, for having any investment case, the yield should be adequate. At present, this happens to be around ~6.46% as highlighted earlier. This is decent, but considering the nature of the entity to operate as a trust, these dividends would most probably also be considered as an ordinary income instead of being treated as qualified dividends for tax purposes. This tends to make the after-tax yield less attractive versus dividends of other entities paying similar in distribution yield, but operating as a corporation allowing the preferred holders to have advantage of income being treated as qualified in nature and taxed at a concessional rate.

A ~6.46% Yield, which happens to be roughly ~2% over the current risk-free rates of ~4.36%, would therefore only make sense when accompanied by adequate safety and an optionality of the capital gains, if any. With reference to preference dividend coverage, the company had a FFO of around ~$2.4 billion after paying out preference dividends. The preferred dividends paid for the year FY 25 were ~$40.7 million. On that aspect, the safety and the underlying cushion appears to be fairly stable at the time being. The FFO further saw a growth of ~18% from its prior year, again reflecting on the strong demand prospects, which was highlighted above. However, still, any deterioration in safety should actively prompt the investor to action. Also, like other fixed income products, this can form part of the overall fixed portfolio allocation for an investor, and should not be overallocated, considering also that the underlying upside stands to be limited at large.

With reference to capital appreciation, all of the three preferred series outstanding, Series J, K, and L are currently redeemable at the company's option. The Series K and Series L no-call windows expired on March 13, 2024 and October 10, 2024 respectively, while the Series J no-call period ended earlier, on August 7, 2022. Simply put, all of the three outstanding listed preference shares for the company are at presently freely callable anytime at company’s option at the price of $25.

Like I highlighted earlier since the gap between par and market price is greater in case of Series J and Series L, that is what I would consider to be worthy of allocation if any. The preferred shares of these features, having no maturity date but an option by the company to redeem at par, reflects negative convexity on the upside. This implies that although they have fair amount of sensitivity with reference to interest rates and decline in market price with the rising of market rates, but at the same time, the appreciation to market price is largely limited to the price in which company can redeem the issue, which many a times, happens to be simply at par. As we might infer that the dividend yield is almost identical in all of these preferred. Also, Series J and Series L preferred are trading at around ~20, highlighting approximately ~25% gap from par value of $25. This directly caps the upside for the issue, closely to the par value, hence the reason for selecting to go with Series J and Series L primarily.

That said, however since the preferred saw a decline in market price due to recent interest rate hikes for the past some time, an investor still has a meaningful scope of capital appreciation and to reap the benefit in form of capital appreciation, in addition to the dividend income in case of declining interest rates.

The point with refence to interest rate which I keep highlighting is that the expectations of further rate hikes on a structural basis from present levels is already low. The differences in opinion are largely between either the rate cuts to remain at the same levels or possibly decline in near to mid-term basis. This aspect, increases the appeal of virtually all of the duration sensitive asset class which at the same time are yielding adequately with reference to their credit profiles. In the recent summary projection by FOMC, we can see that expectations for further rate hike stands significantly low.

In that sense if an investor considers a ~6%-6.5% yield enough with reference to recurring income, the interest rate trajectory enhances the appeal and is often the factor which leads for an investor to commit. However, at the same time, I keep emphasizing that it should be largely considered as an optional incremental factor and not the primary criteria to have exposure on interest rates.

Risk Aspects

The preferred securities rank lower than the $18 billion of fixed debt obligations of the company. The current weighted coupon is remarkably lower, post maturity of debts, it is possible that the coupon gets restated upwards, which in combination with any unforeseen negative business development can materially impact the cash flow generation and debt servicing capacity of the company. That development therefore needs to be monitored from time to time. An investor in addition, in lieu of a ~3.5% incremental yield over common trades away all of the upside which might be possible for the entity considered strong demand prospects. Further in case of declining rates, company might probably redeem the issue, having investor to look out further opportunities and also capping the benefit from price appreciation resulting from declining of market rates. At last, this should be considered along with other preferred issues of different issuers and industries to offset any sector specific concentration risk. When the reward is capped, and income is identical, spreading of holdings across the variety of issuers and sectors is more rational choice.

In Conclusion

The preferred shares of DLR particular of J and L series (DLR.PR.J) and (DLR.PR.L) are rated a buy at present. The strong demand outlook, with stable debt maturity profile and a decent contracted rents in form of increasing backlogs increases the qualitative profile of both common stocks and preference shares. Any probable decline in interest rate can possibly lead to enhanced total returns in addition to the income yield. DLR preferred presently offers a dividend yield of ~6.46% and can be considered as a complimentary allocation for investors looking for a diversified fixed income portfolio.

data.title: Roivant Sciences: Moderna Settlement And Brepocitinib Support A Long-Term Buy
data.description:
Roivant Sciences Ltd. (ROIV) is a late-stage biotechnology company that operates through subsidiaries called Vants. Its current pipeline is centered on immunology and specialty diseases. ROIV also has brepocitinib, a TYK2/JAK1 inhibitor being developed by Priovant for dermatomyositis, non-infectious uveitis, cutaneous sarcoidosis, and lichen planopilaris. Aside from that, ROIV’s mosliciguat is an inhaled sGC activator for pulmonary hypertension associated with interstitial lung disease. And ROIV’s Immunovant has other FcRn programs, including IMVT-1402 and batoclimab. Recently, they settled with Moderna (MRNA) about their COVID-19 patents, giving ROIV a first payment of $950 million in July 2026 and a potential second payment of $1.3 billion. As such, while my bull case has largely played out, I believe the stock is now well-positioned to deliver consistent shareholder value for investors with a long-term time horizon.

A Successful Vant Story

Roivant Sciences Ltd. is a biotech company that develops medicines through its specialized subsidiaries, known as Vants. Each Vant focuses on a specific therapeutic area to speed up drug development. ROIV was founded back in 2014 and is based in London, with offices in New York and Basel, Switzerland. I’ve been following ROIV for a while now. And since my latest November 2025 article, the stock has appreciated another 32.2%. In fact, the stock is up around 150.9% from the first time I covered it, so now I feel it’s appropriate to write another update to my bull case.

Currently, ROIV is focused on immunology and specialty diseases. One of its main drugs is brepocitinib, a TYK2/JAK1 inhibitor developed by Priovant for inflammatory conditions. This asset targets conditions like dermatomyositis, non-infectious uveitis, and cutaneous sarcoidosis. And an important upcoming brepocitinib milestone is for dermatomyositis, a rare autoimmune disease that mainly affects muscles and skin. The drug has an FDA Orphan Drug Designation for this condition. Fortunately, its NDA submission was accepted for priority review, so if approved, the drug could launch by September 2026.

Moreover, dermatomyositis represents a significant opportunity because patients have few treatment options and serious health needs. About 40,000 patients are currently treated, but the total number may be higher. Brepocitinib could offer patients with uncontrolled dermatomyositis an oral option that targets inflammation and may reduce their need for steroids or other challenging treatments. This alone would help ROIV tap into a sizeable TAM that’s projected to reach $1.3 billion by 2030.

Brepocitinib is also being tested in Phase 2b/3 for lichen planopilaris (LPP). This is probably ROIV’s most interesting and newly added indication for its lead asset. LPP is a disease with no FDA-approved treatments that can cause permanent scarring hair loss. LPP may affect up to 100,000 adults in the US. Plus, brepocitinib’s Phase 2b/3 could also potentially move directly to a pivotal trial if it corroborates its earlier positive data.

And given LPP is a huge demographic with few treatment alternatives, I imagine ROIV’s brepocitinib would probably secure a competitive advantage with pricing power in this niche. But more importantly, I think this now validates my initial thesis that brepocitinib would grow from a DM/NIU/CS asset into a broader immunology franchise by tapping into new indications over time.

Aside from that, brepocitinib is in Phase 3 for non-infectious uveitis, with topline data expected by 2H2026. You see, this condition is typically treated with corticosteroids and immunosuppressants. Additionally, there is AbbVie’s (ABBV) Humira, which is approved for some forms of non-infectious uveitis. However, patients often struggle whenever their doctors try to taper their steroid regimens. ROIV’s NEPTUNE trial showed that brepocitinib steroid taper was over twice as fast compared to Humira’s VISUAL 1 trial results.

As such, I think NEPTUNE’s results are interesting, but note that it wasn’t an explicit head-to-head comparison trial between brepocitinib and Humira. Still, we do know that ABBV’s trial with Humira had a median time to treatment failure of 5.6 months versus 3.0 months for placebo. By comparison, ROIV’s trial with brepocitinib had a median time to treatment failure greater than 12 months. Naturally, ROIV still needs to go ahead with a Phase 3 for this indication, but brepocitinib’s Phase 2 definitely seems promising even under a faster steroid taper.

Similarly, brepocitinib for cutaneous sarcoidosis is another orphan inflammatory disease. Here, ROIV completed a Phase 2 trial, and a new Phase 3 is expected to start after finalizing discussions with the FDA. Still, its Phase 2 results here were positive as well, with relevant improvements as measured by the Cutaneous Sarcoidosis Activity and Morphology Instrument (CSAMI) scale. The CSAMI evaluates disease activity for active inflammation and disease damage for scarring or structural damage after inflammation. Patients receiving placebo were flat to slightly worse, while brepocitinib patients improved by about 20 points on the CSAMI scale.

Other Assets and Moderna Settlement

Therefore, as you can see, ROIV’s brepocitinib has quickly become a franchise asset that’s continuously adding new indications. But ROIV also has other noteworthy candidates in its pipeline, like mosliciguat. This is an inhaled sGC activator for pulmonary hypertension associated with interstitial lung disease (PH-ILD), which Pulmovant is developing. Mosliciguat is in Phase 2b, with topline data expected by 2H2026. Phase 1 showed a peak pulmonary vascular resistance (PVR) reduction of 38%, and the safety profile was positive.

Lastly, other important pipeline candidates are IMVT-1402, developed by Immunovant for Graves' disease. A registrational trial is expected to have a readout in 2027. Additionally, Immunovant lists batoclimab in its pipeline. This is a fully human monoclonal antibody that blocks FcRn and may help treat IgG-mediated autoimmune diseases like Graves’ disease, myasthenia gravis (MG), and chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (CIDP).

Having said that, my initial bull case also hinged on ROIV settling its COVID-19 patent litigations, as I saw undervalued optionality on this angle as well. In that sense, ROIV reached a $2.25 billion global settlement with Moderna (MRNA) over MRNA’s COVID-19 vaccines and its lipid nanoparticle (LNP) technology. This settlement includes a first payment of $950 million due in July 2026 and a possible second payment of $1.3 billion, depending on court decisions.

Management mentioned that if they get the full amount, it would become one of the largest patent settlements ever. And ROIV plans to reinvest those funds into its pipeline to support new product launches and buy back up to $1 billion. But, while this resolves ROIV’s litigation with MRNA, its case against Pfizer/BioNTech (PFE) remains open. This was also over the COVID-19 vaccines, and given that ROIV settled favorably with MRNA, I think it bodes well for ROIV in this case as well.

Valuation and Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, ROIV currently trades at a $20.0 billion market cap. When I first covered it, ROIV was only $8.7 billion. So, while the future undoubtedly remains promising, I think the upside is no longer as clear-cut as it was back then. You see, I believe ROIV’s value lies mostly in its potential to sell its assets through M&A deals with its Vants and then either reinvest in its pipeline or repurchase its stock. That’s why I normally anchor my valuation on them by looking at their cash and book value.

As of December 2025, ROIV’s cash balances were $1.5 billion, with $3.1 billion in short-term marketable securities. That amounts to $4.6 billion in liquid funds against no financial debt (aside from other regular operating liabilities). And I calculate their 2025 cash burn was approximately $766.9 million. Note that I got that figure by simply adding its TTM cash flows from operations and CAPEX, which implies a comfortable runway of 6.0 years. Besides, its book value reached $5.0 billion, implying a 4.0 P/B. For comparison, its sector’s median P/B is closer to 2.7, so I’d argue that ROIV’s multiples already have an embedded premium at these levels.

Naturally, those figures ignore the value of the expanding brepocitinib franchise, IMVT-1402, and mosliciguat. Combined, I think these assets will probably generate several billion in revenues over time. ROIV hasn’t given us a precise peak sales figure for these assets, but management’s January 2026 presentation shows they’re projecting billions in sales over the next decade. I imagine their next ER in late May will expand on what they intend to do with brepocitinib, IMVT-1402, and mosliciguat. Personally, I’d like management to fully break down their revenue expectations going forward. After all, they’re clearly signaling a pivot towards becoming a “real company” instead of relying on M&A to monetize their pipeline as they’ve done historically.

However, that’s also the main drawback with ROIV today, as it’s now a bet on management executing on their revenue ramp-up goals. And I feel this makes ROIV better suited for long-term investors who are willing to ride the stock through many years while management delivers on its vision. This is unlike when I first covered ROIV, because back then I thought it was a catalyst-rich stock that was clearly undervalued even by conventional metrics. Plus, at the time, ROIV had a much wider margin of safety. Today, it’s trading at a premium instead.

Moreover, a key challenge for launching brepocitinib will be building patient services and securing reimbursement. In other words, ROIV will need to identify patients, create support programs, educate referral centers and doctors, and help patients get coverage. Management also mentioned they expect the launch to be gradual, with adoption increasing over time. And brepocitinib itself has its drawbacks because trials showed it had infection risks, cardiovascular events, and malignancy concerns. However, the benefit is that brepocitinib reduces steroid use while making its indications manageable for patients. But that’s a tradeoff that physicians will ultimately have to weigh before treating their patients with brepocitinib.

Conclusion: Still A Buy, But Not A Clear Bargain

Overall, ROIV’s Vant model has now yielded 3 incredibly valuable assets with brepocitinib, IMVT-1402, and mosliciguat. They’re also cash-rich and have no financial debts, with a comfortable cash runway for the next several years. ROIV also settled with MRNA regarding its COVID-19 patents, and I believe their case against PFE also looks quite solid. However, ROIV is also now a much more mature company, unlike a couple of years ago, when brepocitinib was still seeking FDA approval and its other R&D programs were in earlier stages. ROIV’s valuation also appears to be pricing in considerable growth in the coming years, meaning that the easy money on the stock is probably done by now. Still, for long-term investors willing to see through management’s revenue ramp-up goals, I feel ROIV still remains a viable long-term “Buy” at these levels.

Investing Environment

Global equities declined during Q1 2026, pulling back from all-time highs as markets responded to a combination of geopolitical escalation, shifting monetary policy expectations and heightened macroeconomic uncertainty. Energy stocks surged, with Q1 returns exceeding 30% across major indices. Value outperformed growth as investors continued to rotate out of technology stocks and into more commodity-sensitive areas.

Market leadership continued to broaden during the period. In the US, large-cap growth stocks lagged, while mid- and small-cap indices were more resilient, posting modest gains for the quarter. The best performing segment of the market was the Russell 2000® Value Index, which delivered a 5.0% return during Q1. Outside the US, emerging markets continued their recent streak of outperformance relative to developed markets. Europe declined, while other developed markets posted mixed results.

Bond markets experienced increased volatility as rising energy prices and shifting monetary policy expectations drove a sharp increase in 10-year Treasury yields, which spiked to 4.4% in late March. Yields later moderated as investors weighed slowing growth against persistent inflation pressures. At its March monetary policy meeting, the Federal Reserve held rates steady and updated its projections to reflect slower growth and somewhat higher inflation. While the Fed continued to characterize the economy as resilient, it emphasized elevated uncertainty and signaled a more cautious, data-dependent approach to policy easing. At the start of Q1, markets expected one or two more rate cuts by the Fed in 2026, but that changed to a modest probability of policy tightening as markets moved to a more balanced view of inflation and growth.

The escalation of conflict in Iran became a central driver of market behavior. Military actions and subsequent retaliations contributed to a sharp rise in oil prices and disrupted supplies of other important industrial inputs. Renewed commodity-driven inflation pressures increased market sensitivity to macroeconomic developments and reinforced a rotation toward commodity-sensitive and defensive parts of the equity market, while adding further pressure to growth-oriented sectors.

Artificial intelligence (AI)-related investment remained an important support for capital spending and corporate earnings. However, investor sentiment toward AI-related equities was uneven, with periodic reassessments of valuation, capital intensity and return potential contributing to increased dispersion within the technology sector. Expanding AI capabilities sent software shares tumbling as investors reassessed business models, competitive positioning and long-term monetization potential. These developments also prompted broader questions around valuation and sustainability across both public and private software markets, contributing to increased volatility within the industry. At the same time, leadership within AI has shifted toward infrastructure-oriented segments such as memory and optical components, which continue to benefit from sustained data center investment. While the mega-cap AI winners that have driven market returns in recent periods lagged during Q1, AI-related spending trends remain intact, supporting continued demand across the broader ecosystem.

Economic data reflected a mixed global backdrop. While US economic activity remained relatively resilient, there were signs of softening in the labor market alongside a notable decline in consumer sentiment. Outside the US, growth remained uneven, with the outlook for Europe revised down following the escalation of conflict and conditions across Asia looking more mixed. Taken together, these dynamics reinforced a more cautious tone in markets as the quarter unfolded.

Performance Discussion

The portfolio declined during the quarter and underperformed the MSCI ACWI Index. Negative stock selection in consumer discretionary, communication services and financials drove relative underperformance. This was partially offset by strong selection in industrials and a slight overweight to energy, as market leadership evolved.

The quarter unfolded in two distinct phases. Performance in January and February was driven by weakness in consumer Internet holdings. Several of our strongest performers from 2024 and 2025 (Tencent (TCEHY), Sea (SE), Shopify (SHOP), Amazon (AMZN)) sold off amid broad concerns around AI disintermediation. This weakness persisted into March, as these same businesses faced additional pressure given their exposure to consumer spending, leading to further multiple compression. Collectively, these holdings were among the largest drags on performance.

As the quarter progressed, portfolio positioning proved more resilient, and our performance partially recovered. A shift in market leadership following the onset of the Middle East conflict benefited defense and more inflation-sensitive exposures. Our defense holdings, which represent 12% of the portfolio, contributed meaningfully. Companies such as RTX (RTX) and L3Harris Technologies (LHX) rose on increased demand visibility in a missile-based conflict environment. Energy and power-related exposures also supported results. Holdings such as Baker Hughes (BKR) and GE Vernova (GEV) benefited from strength in energy markets, while inflation-insulated businesses like Linde (LIN) provided additional stability.

Within information technology, performance was mixed. Semiconductor and infrastructure-related holdings, including Lam Research (LRCX) and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSMC)(TSM), contributed positively due to AI-related capital spending, while software and IT services names detracted. Additionally, select data and information services businesses, including S&P Global (SPGI) and RELX (RELX), detracted as concerns about AI disruption intensified. Financials were also a source of weakness, with all holdings declining and most detracting from relative results.

Among our top contributors in Q1 were GE Vernova, L3Harris Technologies and Linde. GE Vernova is the power, wind and electrification spinoff from the former GE conglomerate. Shares rose following a solid quarterly report. Among its segments, power exceeded expectations, and electrification remained strong due to data center demand, though this was partially offset by ongoing challenges in its wind segment. Margin expansion is gaining traction as higher priced orders flow through and operational efficiencies improve, supported by strong pricing and a growing service base.

L3Harris, a global aerospace and defense company, remains well positioned to benefit from growing investment in next-generation missile defense and national security programs. Recent results were in line with expectations, and management reaffirmed its outlook following a transition to a simplified segment structure and GAAP reporting framework. Order activity remained solid across missile defense, space sensing, communications, and intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance (ISR), although near-term revenue was modestly affected by the timing of government awards.

Linde is the world's largest industrial gas company. It benefits from high visibility, pricing power and resilient earnings supported by long-term contracts and an oligopolistic industry structure. Recent results were modestly ahead of expectations, and management maintained a conservative outlook, despite early signs of improvement in key end markets. While near-term growth remains muted, improving dynamics for helium and a gradual recovery in industrial activity support a path to earnings acceleration. Longer term drivers, including electronics demand and the expansion of its space-related business, reinforce the potential durability of its profit cycle. We added to the position, based on improving fundamentals for helium and increasing demand from rocket launches, where Linde is a primary supplier.

Among our biggest detractors in Q1 were Tencent, Shopify and Amazon. Tencent is a leading Chinese Internet platform anchored by its WeChat ecosystem, which includes social, gaming, advertising and payments. Shares declined as the company signaled increased investment in AI. This raised concerns around near-term margin pressure and the duration of the investment cycle. While underlying results remained solid, we trimmed the position as the company enters a more capital-intensive phase.

Shopify's global commerce platform enables merchants to manage their businesses across channels. Shares declined after the company guided to lower-than-expected incremental free cash flow margins for Q1 2026. However, Shopify continues to gain share, with gross merchandise volume increasing 31% in the prior quarter. We believe the platform remains under-monetized relative to the value it provides. Recent investments are driving momentum, and the company is demonstrating leadership in agentic product innovation. We added to the position on weakness.

Amazon, one of the world's largest online retailers and a leading provider of enterprise cloud services, also detracted despite delivering solid operating results. Amazon Web Services' growth accelerated to 24%, while retail and advertising remained strong. However, shares declined due to a meaningful increase in planned capital expenditures and modest near-term margin pressure related to low Earth orbit satellite investments as the company looks to compete with SpaceX through Project Kuiper. We view these investments as well supported by strong compute demand and Amazon's proven execution in cloud services. We expect Trainium, the company's custom chips, to enhance long-term margins and competitive positioning. We added to the position on share price weakness.

Portfolio Activity

During the quarter, we initiated new positions in Edwards Lifesciences, Eli Lilly and Roblox. Edwards Lifesciences (EW) designs, develops, manufactures and markets products and services for the treatment of late-stage cardiovascular disease. The company is a long-standing leader in heart valve replacement, particularly minimally invasive, catheter-based solutions. We initiated a position as the core aortic valve franchise reaccelerates and demand for newer tricuspid valve therapies is growing rapidly, expanding Edwards' addressable market. This next product cycle is supported by strong clinical data and increasing physician adoption, with Edwards gaining share as competing solutions struggle to match its outcomes. Over time, potential for workflow simplification, including greater procedural autonomy for cardiologists, could improve capacity and utilization, supporting both volume growth and margin expansion. Following several years of elevated investment, we expect stronger revenue growth to drive margin expansion.

Eli Lilly (LLY) is a leading pharmaceutical company with a dominant position in the GLP-1 market across injectable and oral therapies. We opportunistically initiated a Garden SM position following a recent share price pullback but ahead of the anticipated approval and launch of Foundayo ® and a potential expansion of Medicare coverage for obesity drugs. Lower pricing and broader formulary inclusion are expanding the addressable market, particularly as compounded alternatives become less competitive. Strong category demand and Eli Lilly's broad portfolio position the company well to drive accelerating obesity volumes. We view Foundayo ® as a best-in-class oral GLP-1 medicine based on clinical data and early indicators suggesting strong adoption potential. To date, we have not observed meaningful cannibalization of existing injectable therapies and believe market expansion can support both modalities. Early prescription trends represent a key near-term catalyst.

Roblox (RBLX) is a leading user-generated content platform with strong global growth and operating leverage, supported by AI-driven innovation and infrastructure advantages. We initiated a Garden SM position as the stock appears to be pricing in a sharp deceleration in growth, elevated investment spending and increased competitive risks, despite stable underlying engagement and bookings. We believe Roblox's developer ecosystem, global distribution and social network effects create a differentiated platform with long-term monetization potential. We view concerns around emerging AI competition as overstated. Following the recent pullback, we see a disconnect between valuation and fundamentals, with significant risk already reflected in the share price.

In addition to Linde (LIN), Shopify (SHOP) and Amazon (AMZN), we also added to Woodward (WWD) and Spotify (SPOT) during the quarter. Woodward is a leading supplier of mission-critical motion and control systems, with approximately two-thirds of its revenue derived from aerospace and defense. The company is well positioned to benefit from a multiyear commercial aerospace upcycle, supported by a large installed base, a significant backlog and increasing shop visits, which remain below 2019 levels. Woodward's content on next-generation LEAP engines is materially higher while flight hours and fleet sizes are expected to expand, which should drive outsized growth over the next five to eight years. The industrial segment has also turned a corner with demand accelerating across power generation, oil and gas, and marine end markets. We increased the position as conviction in both the aerospace aftermarket cycle and the industrial recovery strengthened.

Spotify, a leading audio streaming platform, is well positioned to further monetize its dominant role in global music distribution and discovery through subscription pricing, advertising and tiered subscription offerings. We believe the upcoming super-premium tier represents a meaningful catalyst. In its most recent quarter, Spotify delivered solid user growth and operating leverage, with revenue and operating income exceeding guidance. Management highlighted improving gross margin trends and reiterated its confidence in its AI-enabled roadmap, including deeper integration with large language models and continued expansion of its advertising network. While shares declined amid broader multiple compression and concerns around advertising trends, underlying fundamentals are improving. We view Spotify's recommendation engine as a key competitive advantage, enabling personalized discovery in an increasingly abundant content ecosystem. In this context, we see AI as a reinforcing, rather than disruptive, force. We added to the position on recent weakness.

We ended our investment campaigns in Netflix (NFLX), Snowflake (SNOW) and RELX (RELX) during the quarter. Netflix is the world's largest subscription video-on-demand service. We exited the position as the investment thesis matured and incremental upside became increasingly dependent on later-cycle execution. Recent strategic actions, including a bid to acquire Warner Bros Discovery assets, suggest a shift toward greater complexity and balance sheet risk. While no transaction occurred, these developments, along with increased investment spending, indicate a potential change in management philosophy. As the business enters a new phase, we chose to redeploy capital into higher conviction opportunities.

Snowflake is a leading player in data infrastructure, enabling organizations to unlock greater value from their data estates. We exited the position following strong performance, as our conviction in its role as a core enabler of AI-driven workloads declined. As AI adoption has progressed, we have increasingly questioned Snowflake's positioning within the data stack, particularly as alternative platforms demonstrate stronger growth and large language models (LLMs) enable more direct use of unstructured data. This uncertainty, combined with moderating growth and uneven consumption trends, reduced our confidence in the durability of its long-term growth profile.

RELX is a global provider of information-based analytics and decision tools with leading positions across risk, scientific, legal and exhibitions markets. We exited the position as concerns around AI-driven disruption to core legal workflows increased, particularly within its legal research platform. AI-native competitors are beginning to target front-end legal workflows, introducing greater uncertainty about the company's long-term growth and the durability of its subscription model. As a result, we continued to consolidate our AI exposure into a smaller set of higher conviction, more resilient franchises.

In addition to Tencent (TCEHY), other notable trims during the quarter were S&P Global (SPGI), Lam Research (LRCX) and Lonza Group (LZAGY). S&P Global is one of the largest credit ratings agencies globally and a provider of benchmarks, data and analytics to the global capital and commodities markets. We began to harvest the position due to AI-driven disintermediation risks. We believe parts of the company, particularly its market intelligence segment and acquired data assets, are more vulnerable to disruption from LLMs. While the ratings business remains highly durable, recent results have shown slower growth in certain areas. We chose to reallocate capital toward higher conviction opportunities within data and market infrastructure.

Lam is a leading supplier of semiconductor manufacturing equipment, with dominant positions in etching and deposition. The company delivered results that met elevated expectations, with margins holding up better than anticipated and guidance implying continued momentum. Lam is a key beneficiary of semiconductor capital spending growth, particularly in memory. Given strong share price performance, cyclical exposure and valuation approaching the upper end of our range, we trimmed the position to manage risk and position size.

Lonza is a global leader in pharmaceutical contract development and manufacturing, with a strong position in biologics manufacturing. We reduced the position as the ramp of its Vacaville facility has progressed more slowly than expected, and additional industry capacity in the US, particularly from large-scale peers, is likely to moderate market share gains and extend the timeline to full utilization. Although underlying demand remains strong, we see fewer near-term catalysts and are prioritizing capital toward higher conviction health care opportunities earlier in their profit cycles.

Perspective

Early 2026 was marked by geopolitical shocks and heightened volatility, with global equities declining modestly over the quarter. Despite this backdrop, pockets of strength emerged across energy, utilities, materials, consumer staples and industrials, helping to offset broader weakness. Equity markets entered the year near record levels but faced pressure from rising geopolitical tensions, energy price shocks and shifting monetary policy expectations. At the same time, market leadership continued to expand beyond a narrow group of large-cap technology stocks, with performance increasingly supported by secular trends outside of AI. Notably, each member of the Magnificent Seven underperformed the S&P 500® Index during the quarter.

We believe this environment remains supportive of our investment process, which emphasizes bottom-up stock selection across industries. Periods characterized by broader market participation and greater dispersion of returns have historically created more opportunities for differentiated research to add value. While sentiment can shift quickly in response to macro developments, we maintain our belief that, over time, stock prices are driven by earnings, and durable profit cycles remain the most reliable source of long-term outcomes. As noted earlier, we are beginning to see evidence that secular growth drivers beyond AI are gaining traction, reinforcing our view that a more balanced opportunity set is emerging.

Within information technology, portfolio positioning has evolved meaningfully over the past several years. We believe our exposure is now better aligned with areas of durable demand, particularly within AI-related infrastructure. Holdings such as Lam Research, TSMC, Broadcom and Advanced Micro Devices provide exposure to semiconductor equipment, connectivity and power-related demand tied to the build-out of AI capabilities. At the same time, we have reduced or eliminated exposure to software business models that appear more vulnerable to disruption, and we see little evidence of "AI roadkill" within the portfolio today.

Health care remains a key area of strength and conviction. Pharmaceutical holdings such as AstraZeneca and UCB contributed meaningfully to performance, reflecting continued progress across underlying product cycles. More broadly, we remain optimistic about our large health care portfolio given the combination of company-specific growth drivers, limited economic sensitivity and generally lower AI disruption risk. In our view, much of the sector's value remains anchored in biology, regulation and physical infrastructure, where substitution risk is limited. We continue to see a compelling opportunity set as innovation translates into durable earnings growth.

We also remain attracted to aerospace and defense, where we see a structural shift driven by modernization and global rearmament. Governments are not only responding to near-term conflicts but also rebuilding inventories, upgrading capabilities and establishing higher baseline levels of readiness. This is supporting sustained demand across mission-critical technologies, including sensing, propulsion, communications and missile defense systems. In addition, commercial aerospace continues to benefit from strong travel demand, aging fleets and the need for more fuel-efficient aircraft. We see attractive multiyear growth opportunities in companies such as L3Harris Technologies and Woodward, discussed earlier, as well as BAE Systems and RTX.

Within consumer-oriented areas, we continue to favor platform-based Internet businesses over more traditional retail models. These companies have become more attractively valued in recent months, with lower expectations and improving business quality. We maintain limited exposure to brick-and-mortar retail and remain more constructive on consumer Internet platforms that benefit from scalable models and durable competitive advantages.

While macroeconomic and policy uncertainty remain elevated, we continue to have confidence in the portfolio's company-specific growth drivers. As such, periods of heightened volatility tend to create opportunities in high-quality franchises now trading at more attractive valuations. Consistent with our process, we will look to take advantage of near-term trading dynamics to position the portfolio for potential attractive long-term returns.
